retrieved_docs.title: Pain Management Medications -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Nonopioid analgesic agents

retrieved_docs.title: Misoprostol -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Misoprostol is a synthetic prostaglandin E1 analog that inhibits basal and nocturnal gastric acid secretion by directly stimulating prostaglandin E1 receptors on parietal cells in the stomach. This action reduces gastric acid secretion triggered by food, alcohol, NSAIDs, histamine, and caffeine, with a dose-dependent relationship.

retrieved_docs.title: Misoprostol -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: NSAID-induced gastritis is a common issue encountered across various medical specialties. Evidence suggests that strategies to prevent NSAID-induced gastritis are often underutilized, particularly in patients at risk for gastrointestinal complications related to NSAID therapy. A retrospective observational cohort study revealed that 86.6% of new NSAID users with 1 risk factor for upper gastrointestinal ulcers and 81.2% with 2 risk factors did not receive appropriate prophylaxis. [34] Experts believe better compliance with this principle can be achieved through better education for both clinicians and patients. [35] A gastroenterologist should be consulted for NSAID-induced ulcers that present with complications.

retrieved_docs.title: Misoprostol -- Contraindications -- Warnings and Precautions
retrieved_docs.content: Misoprostol is contraindicated in individuals with a known allergy or hypersensitivity to prostaglandins. [22] [29] Patients at risk for gastric ulcers due to NSAID use who are pregnant should avoid misoprostol because of its potential to cause serious adverse effects during pregnancy.

retrieved_docs.title: Pain Management Medications -- Contraindications -- Nonopioid analgesic agents
retrieved_docs.content: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) : Hypersensitivity is the only major contraindication for NSAID use. However, multiple conditions demand avoidance, temporary suspension, or extremely cautious use, as follows: Age > 50 years and family history of gastrointestinal (GI) disease/bleeding. Previous GI problems associated with NSAID use (eg., gastritis) Peptic ulcer History of personal GI bleeding Uncontrolled hypertension Renal disease Irritable bowel syndrome Inflammatory bowel disease Coronary artery bypass surgery Gastric bypass surgery Pregnancy (third trimester) Stroke (excluding aspirin) Transient ischemic attack (excluding aspirin) Myocardial infarction (excluding aspirin) Congestive heart failure (excluding aspirin) [7] [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Nonsteroidal Anti-Inflammatory Drugs Toxicity -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The most common adverse effects of NSAIDs involve the GI system, including risks of ulceration, bleeding, or perforation. While these risks can occur at any time in patients of any age, they are more prevalent in the older population. Other undesirable GI events may include nausea, dyspepsia, loss of appetite, abdominal pain, and diarrhea resulting from erosion of the alimentary canal. Prostanoids, such as PGE2 and PGI2, contribute to GI mucous secretion and promote vasodilation, thereby enhancing blood flow and bicarbonate delivery to mucosal surfaces. Inhibition of COX-1 reduces these cytoprotective effects.

retrieved_docs.title: Misoprostol -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Misoprostol is a synthetic prostaglandin E1 analog approved by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for preventing and treating gastric ulcers induced by nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs). Misoprostol has 4 primary effects, which include cytoprotection of the gastrointestinal mucosa, uterotonic properties, and the adverse effects of diarrhea, abdominal pain, and nausea. Although not FDA-approved, misoprostol is indicated for the short-term treatment of active duodenal or gastric ulcers caused by factors other than NSAIDs.

retrieved_docs.title: Nonsteroidal Anti-Inflammatory Drugs Toxicity -- Toxicokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Most commonly, the risk of severe GI adverse effects, including ulceration, bleeding, or perforation, is increased with NSAID consumption. Though these risks can occur at any time in patients of any age, these adverse events tend to present more commonly in the elderly. Other undesirable GI events may include nausea, dyspepsia, loss of appetite, abdominal pain, and diarrhea from erosion of the alimentary canal. It is well understood that prostanoids such as PGE2 and PGI2 constitutively contribute to GI mucous secretion. Additionally, these prostanoids promote vasodilation, allowing for enhanced blood flow and bicarbonate delivery to mucosal surfaces. Primary inhibition of COX-1 reduces these cytoprotective effects.

retrieved_docs.title: Misoprostol -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: According to the American College of Gastroenterology guidelines, gastrointestinal adverse effects and frequent dosing limit the use of misoprostol. Treatment with omeprazole effectively prevents recurrent ulcer bleeding in patients using NSAIDs who also have a concomitant Helicobacter pylori infection. As a result, proton pump inhibitors are commonly used for the prophylaxis and treatment of NSAID-related upper gastrointestinal injuries. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Misoprostol -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Misoprostol is a synthetic prostaglandin E1 analog approved by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for preventing and treating gastric ulcers induced by nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs). This analog inhibits basal and nocturnal gastric acid secretion through direct stimulation of prostaglandin E1 receptors on parietal cells in the stomach.

retrieved_docs.title: Pain Management Medications -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: For a complete list of adverse effects for a particular NSAID, please see the StatPearls article for the specific drug.

retrieved_docs.title: Misoprostol -- Adverse Effects -- Drug-Drug Interactions
retrieved_docs.content: Antacids: Concurrent use of misoprostol with magnesium-containing antacids may increase the incidence of diarrhea.

retrieved_docs.title: Misoprostol -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Misoprostol is FDA-approved solely for the prevention and treatment of NSAID-induced gastric ulcers in patients at high risk for ulceration. Misoprostol has 4 primary effects—cytoprotection of the gastrointestinal mucosa, uterotonic properties, diarrhea, and abdominal pain—the latter 2 are considered adverse effects. [1] [2] Although not FDA-approved, misoprostol is also indicated for the short-term treatment of active duodenal or gastric ulcers caused by factors other than NSAIDs. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Nonsteroidal Anti-Inflammatory Drugs Toxicity -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the various pharmacologic mechanisms by which NSAIDs can cause toxicity. Apply best practices when evaluating a patient who may be showing signs of NSAID toxicity. Apply evidence-based treatment strategies for NSAID toxicity. Improve care coordination among the interprofessional team to enhance outcomes for patients who either have or are potential candidates for developing NSAID toxicity. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Prostaglandins -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Misoprostol, a PGE1 analog, is approved by the FDA to reduce the risk of nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID)–induced gastric ulcers in patients at high risk for complications. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 994
retrieved_docs.content: Proton pump inhibitors for peptic ulcer and GORD comprise dexlansoprazol, vonoprazan, esomeprazol, lansoprazole, pantoprazole and tegoprazan, all of which are considered members of the therapeutic class used to treat peptic ulcer disease and gastro‑oesophageal reflux disease. These agents act to suppress gastric acid secretion, thereby promoting ulcer healing and symptom relief in GORD, and provide clinicians with a range of options for effective acid‑blocking therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 1181
retrieved_docs.content: Proton pump inhibitors indicated for the treatment of peptic ulcer disease and gastro‑oesophageal reflux disease (GORD) comprise a group of related medications, including dexlansoprazole, vonoprazan, esomeprazole, lansoprazole, and tegoprazan. Each of these agents is a member of the proton pump inhibitor class and is used to reduce gastric acid secretion, thereby promoting healing of peptic ulcers and alleviating the symptoms of GORD. Recognizing that these drugs belong to the same therapeutic category helps clinicians select appropriate acid‑suppression therapy and understand their shared mechanism of action in managing ulcer‑related and reflux conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 104
retrieved_docs.content: Proton pump inhibitors for peptic ulcer and GORD represent a therapeutic class of acid‑suppressing agents used to treat peptic ulcer disease and gastro‑oesophageal reflux disease. This class includes the drugs vonoprazan, esomeprazole, lansoprazole, pantoprazole, rabeprazole and tegoprazan, each of which functions by inhibiting gastric acid secretion. By sharing this common mechanism, all of these agents are employed to promote ulcer healing, relieve reflux symptoms, and prevent recurrence of peptic ulcer and GORD, making them central components of medical management for these conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1304
retrieved_docs.content: Enprostil is classified as a member of the prostaglandin drug group that is used in the management of peptic ulcer disease and gastro‑oesophageal reflux disease (GORD). This relationship indicates that enprostil belongs to the therapeutic class of prostaglandin agents specifically indicated for treating these gastrointestinal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1349
retrieved_docs.content: Proton pump inhibitors for peptic ulcer and GORD constitute a therapeutic class that includes esomeprazol and lanzoprazol as individual members. These agents function by inhibiting gastric H⁺/K⁺‑ATPase, thereby reducing gastric acid secretion, facilitating ulcer healing, and alleviating symptoms of gastro‑oesophageal reflux disease. Their inclusion in treatment regimens reflects their central role in managing acid‑related gastrointestinal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 140
retrieved_docs.content: DRUGS FOR ACID RELATED DISORDERS is a therapeutic category that comprises antacida (e.g., hydroxid hlinitý), H2‑receptor antagonists, proton‑pump inhibitors (PPIs), prostaglandin analogues, and fixed‑dose combination regimens for Helicobacter pylori eradication. Antacida form a subclass of this category. H2‑receptor antagonists are a subclass that includes cimetidine (a member of the guanidin chemical class and the broader histamin H2‑antagonist group), nizatidine, ranitidine bismuth citrate, and roxatidine; all act by blocking histamine‑mediated gastric acid secretion. PPIs for peptic ulcer and gastro‑oesophageal reflux disease (GORD) are defined as ISA drugs and include vonoprazan, esomeprazole, lansoprazole, rabeprazole, tegoprazan, and omeprazole, each irreversibly inhibiting the gastric H⁺/K⁺‑ATPase pump. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1824
retrieved_docs.content: H2‑receptor antagonists constitute a class of drugs that act as histamine H2 antagonists, sharing the broader pharmacologic relationship of inhibiting gastric acid secretion via blockade of the H2 histamine receptor. Specific agents that belong to this class include ranitidine, nizatidine, and roxatidine, each representing an individual member of the H2‑receptor antagonist group. Understanding that these medications are unified by their mechanism of H2‑receptor blockade helps clinicians select appropriate therapy for conditions such as peptic ulcer disease and gastro‑oesophageal reflux, while recognizing the shared therapeutic effects and potential class‑related adverse profiles.

retrieved_docs.content: 2,3‑dihydrothiopyrano(2,3‑b)pyridine and L 750355 are β3‑adrenergic receptor antagonists that share a pyridine scaffold, while the enzyme‑inhibitor drug isbogrel is also derived from the pyridine chemical class, illustrating that pyridine‑based heterocycles can serve distinct pharmacologic roles such as β3‑adrenergic blockade and enzyme inhibition. In the management of acid‑related disorders, drugs for peptic ulcer and gastro‑oesophageal reflux disease (GORD) are organized under the broader category “drugs for acid‑related disorders” and are divided into three therapeutic subclasses. H₂‑receptor antagonists—synonymous with histamine H₂ antagonists—include lafutidine, nizatidine, ranitidine‑bismuth citrate, and roxatidine, each reducing gastric acidity by blocking gastric H₂ receptors. Prostaglandin agents for peptic ulcer and GORD are represented by enprostil, which promotes mucosal protection and healing through prostaglandin‑mediated mechanisms. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 62
retrieved_docs.content: Proton pump inhibitors used for the treatment of peptic ulcer disease and gastro‑oesophageal reflux disease (GORD) comprise a group of medications that share the common purpose of reducing gastric acid secretion. The agents that belong to this therapeutic class include vonoprazan, esomeprazole, lansoprazole, pantoprazole, and tegoprazan. All of these drugs are considered members of the proton pump inhibitor category for peptic ulcer and GORD, providing clinicians with multiple options for acid‑suppression therapy based on efficacy, safety profile, and patient‑specific considerations.
